Title: #NSTviral: Firework malfunction at festive gathering sparks outrage over child safety
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A viral video showing a firework malfunction at a festive gathering has sparked widespread outrage, with netizens condemning the organisers for endangering children.
The 40-second clip on X captures the moment a
festive
dinner in an outdoor dining area turned chaotic,
The firework misfired, shooting high-velocity sparks horizontally into a seated crowd instead of into the sky. Panic ensued as guests, including several young children, scrambled for cover amid thick smoke and deafening explosions.
The footage notably shows a young boy standing just feet away from where the sparks landed, narrowly avoiding serious injury. Later, adults were seen frantically checking on him to ensure he had not sustained burns from the stray embers.
Netizens were quick to condemn the "irresponsible" act of igniting heavy pyrotechnics in a confined and crowded space.
"This is incredibly reckless. Having children so close to a firework cake in a small area is a recipe for tragedy. There is no clear escape route in a crowded setting if a fire breaks out," commented one concerned user.
Another added: "Setting off fireworks right in the line of fire of children is unforgivable. This is a blatant violation of safety protocols."
Experts have long warned that fireworks require a wide, open perimeter and a stable base to prevent tipping or malfunction. Using them in residential settings, especially in enclosed or crowded spaces, poses a severe fire risk and a direct physical threat to bystanders.
As of this afternoon, the video has garnered over 100,000 views, serving as a timely reminder for everyone to remain vigilant and prioritise safety during festive celebrations.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Former TV3 journalist Karam Singh Walia dies
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Renowned environmental journalist Datuk Karam Singh Walia has died.
He died at his home in Klang at 3.30am today.
Known for weaving pantun and proverbs into his investigative reports, Karam was a broadcast journalist for 20 years before retiring in 2014 due to health issues.
In a statement announcing Karam's passing, Media Prima Bhd described him as an  iconic journalist, who was a colleague and friend to many.
"Our condolences, thoughts and prayers are with his family," the company said.
MORE TO COME
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: UNIFIL warns Israel, Hizbollah attacks near its positions risk return fire
Article:
BEIRUT: The United Nations Interim Force in Lebanon said attacks by Israel and Hizbollah near its positions "could potentially draw return fire."
In a statement, UNIFIL said it was "extremely concerned" about attacks from both sides "carried out from near our positions, which could potentially draw return fire."
It urged them to "put down their weapons and work seriously toward a ceasefire."--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: From Market to Manufacturing: Malaysia's EV Investment Reset
Article:
MALAYSIA is drawing a new line in the sand: it will no longer be merely a market for electric vehicles - it intends to be a place where they are built, integrated and exported.
This shift comes at a time when the global EV industry is entering a more difficult phase.
Major manufacturers are cutting prices aggressively - BYD alone reduced prices on up to 22 models by as much as 34 per cent amid rising inventory levels estimated at three to four months of supply.
At the same time, state support remains significant, with subsidies accounting for as much as 38 per cent of net profit for leading players.
Together, these trends point to a global market increasingly shaped not just by innovation, but by excess capacity and policy-driven competition.
It is against this backdrop that Malaysia's evolving investment stance must be understood.
The recent clarifications by Investment, Trade and Industry Ministry particularly in relation to BYD's proposed investment in Tanjung Malim, Perak are not an isolated response.
They are part of a broader policy reset - one that seeks to ensure that foreign investment delivers not just volume, but value.
Rewriting The Terms of Entry
Malaysia remains open to foreign investment. This is evident from the presence of 34 foreign automotive brands in the country, of which 14 are Chinese, alongside recent approvals granted to companies such as BYD and Chery.
The issue is not entry. It is the terms of participation.
Under the current framework, new high-volume automotive investments are expected to meet clear conditions.
These include an export-oriented production model, limits on domestic sales such as the 10,000-unit annual threshold for BYD's local market and requirements for substantive local assembly including body, paint and trim operations.
A minimum on-the-road price of RM100,000 has also been set for locally assembled EVs.
These measures are not arbitrary. They are designed to ensure that Malaysia captures real economic value.
Export orientation enables scale and integrates production into regional supply chains spanning Asean's 600 million consumers.
Local assembly requirements anchor manufacturing activity within the country, moving beyond superficial "screwdriver" operations.
Pricing discipline prevents a purely price-led market entry that could destabilise the domestic ecosystem without generating corresponding industrial benefits.
Equally important is the consistency of application. The ministry has made clear that these conditions are non-discriminatory and apply across the board to new entrants.
For investors, this clarity reduces uncertainty and strengthens confidence in Malaysia's policy environment.
From Volume Growth To Value Capture
This approach reflects a deeper strategic transition. In earlier years, Malaysia's EV policies focused on stimulating demand.
Temporary measures such as lowering the price threshold for imported EVs to RM100,000 between 2022 and 2025 - were intended to accelerate adoption and familiarise consumers with new technologies.
That phase is now giving way to one centred on value capture. The question is no longer how many EVs Malaysia can sell, but how much of the EV value chain it can retain.
The stakes are significant. The domestic automotive ecosystem already supports more than 700,000 jobs and contributes about four per cent to GDP.
National players such as Proton and Perodua have achieved localisation rates exceeding 75 per cent in key models, built over decades of investment and vendor development.
The policy objective is therefore to ensure that new investments reinforce, rather than erode, this foundation.
In practical terms, this means aligning incentives with outcomes. Investments are expected to generate technology transfer, deepen supplier linkages, and create high-skilled employment.
Malaysia's network of 17 free trade agreements and its position at the heart of Asean provide a natural platform for export-oriented production.
The intention is to move from being a terminal market to becoming a regional manufacturing and distribution hub.
Balancing Openness With Industrial Depth
There are, inevitably, trade-offs. More stringent conditions may deter some investors seeking rapid market entry through low-cost strategies.
Consumers may also see a more gradual trajectory in price reductions. Yet these are short-term considerations.
A purely consumption-driven model offers immediate gains but limited structural benefits. In contrast, a production-oriented approach builds capabilities that endure. It creates industries, not just markets.
The real test lies in execution.
Policy clarity must be matched by institutional efficiency.  Approval processes must remain timely. Vendor capabilities must be strengthened to meet localisation requirements.
Skills development must keep pace with technological change, particularly in areas such as battery systems, electronics and advanced manufacturing.
At the same time, Malaysia must remain competitive within the region.
Neighbouring economies are pursuing their own EV strategies, combining incentives with localisation requirements of their own. Malaysia's advantage will depend not only on its policies, but on its ability to deliver a coherent, reliable and business-friendly environment.
What is now clear is the direction of travel.
Malaysia is not retreating from global investment. It is redefining its terms. The message to investors is precise: access to the Malaysian market is no longer the endgame - it is the starting point.
Those who come will find a country ready to partner, to build and to scale. But they will also find a country that expects something in return: commitment, capability, and contribution.
In the years ahead, the winners will not be those who simply sell into markets, but those who embed themselves within them. Malaysia has made its choice.
*The writer is an analyst of global politics, business and economics. He is an adjunct lecturer at Universiti Teknologi Petronas and a senior consultant with Global Asia Consulting. He writes on global perspectives, strategy and statecraft, offering strategic insights for a complex world. The views expressed here are entirely his own.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Bell sees Southeast Asia helicopter market growth driven by GDP expansion
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Bell expects continued growth in commercial and government rotorcraft demand in Southeast Asia as economic expansion increases the number of private operators, enabling governments to increase spending on defence and humanitarian missions.
Bell Asia Pacific managing director David Sale said helicopter market in the region is closely linked to economic growth which remains higher than in Western markets.
"Most countries here are seeing GDP growth of more than five per cent. That's different from the West where you're seeing two to three per cent. So, as the GDP continues to grow here, your middle class starts to grow, your upper class starts to grow."
"With the growth of GDP, governments start having enough money to do things like emergency medical services (EMS) and pair up public operations. You'll see EMS starting to take hold. Southeast Asia is in the infancy of EMS," Sale told Business Times in a recent interview.
He said the offshore oil and gas industry remains a key helicopter market in Asia particularly for deepwater operations in markets such as China and Australia where long-distance offshore flights are required.
Bell is working towards certification of its new Bell 525, which Sale said will be the first fly-by-wire aircraft in the commercial helicopter market once certified and is designed primarily for offshore oil and gas operations.
"It will be the first ever fly-by-wire aircraft in the commercial market once certified, so we're pushing hard with the US Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) to get that aircraft certified. You can go over 500 nautical miles and that is going to provide a capability that's never been provided before," he said.
The aircraft is designed to operate at high utilisation rates of up to nine hours a day with data transmitted from the aircraft to maintenance teams to monitor aircraft systems and components.
Sale also said the Bell 505 is seeing demand across commercial and military segments particularly as a training aircraft.
He said the aircraft is used an as ab initio trainer in several countries including South Korea, Japan, Indonesia and Jordan with 40 aircraft already in service with the South Korean army and navy.
"Across the region, we're about 50-50 split between commercial and military. On the commercial side, it's great for the corporate customers but on the military side, it's a great ab initio trainer or initial training," he added.
Bell, together with its affiliate TRU Simulation + Training Inc, developed the Veris VR Simulator – an FAA Level 7 flight training device that enables pilots to train for a wide range of real-world scenarios with high accuracy including system malfunctions and emergency procedures, ensuring comprehensive preparedness for real-word operations.
"With the combination of the simulator and aircraft, the operational cost of the simulator is low so we can get a little bit more flight time in the simulator and then a little bit less time in the actual aircraft, and we can reduce the cost to actually train a student to fly," Sale said.
Speaking on Malaysia, Bell has been focusing on the commercial segment and recently conducted demonstration flights with the Bell 505 in Kuala Lumpur.
"Malaysia has always been a tough market and we spent a fair amount of time in there. We are continuing to spend time in Malaysia to focus on commercial side," he said.
Sale said as the commercial helicopter market in Southeast Asia expands, education and regulatory coordination are needed to improve public perception and awareness of helicopter operations.
"All of the commercial market or the general aviation market is on the verge of shifting. What I mean by shifting is we see an expansion coming.
"But there's education and regulations that we are all trying to come together as original equipment manufacturers (OEMs) to say how do we ensure that people know how safe it is to fly," he added.
Sale said Bell's strategy in Asia Pacific includes strengthening its regional support network through its facilities and approved maintenance centres as well as customer support engineers located across the region.
"What we find is that gives our customers a reliability rate that is higher than our competition. It gets them (helicopters) back up in the air and they can generate more revenues for their companies or they can make their missions for their governments," he said.
Bell also operates customer support services around the clock to support operators across different time zones.
"We are with our customers. We have a very high readiness rate and we help our customers either generate revenue better than our competition or we get them to make mission because of our local footprint and customer service engineers and we're open 24/7," he added.
On Bell's sales performance in Asia Pacific in 2025, Sale said the company recorded a strong performance including in India and the Philippines, and expects the momentum to continue into 2026.
"We had a strong year. Last year in India alone, we had our strongest year ever. Philippines was also a very strong place for commercial aircraft. Across the board, we had a strong year in 2025 and it sets the foundation for what we're looking for in 2026 and beyond," he said.
Looking ahead, Sale said Bell expects to make several announcements this year.
"We're excited to make a couple big announcements this year throughout some wins that we hope to capture this year. That's what we're looking forward to and to continue supporting our customers," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Adnex maps expansion push post-ACE listing
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Adnex Group Bhd is gearing up for its next phase of expansion following its listing on the ACE Market of Bursa Malaysia on March 17, with fresh proceeds set to drive domestic growth, digital upgrades and regional forays.
Managing director Kan Wai Chun said the listing enables the company to raise funds to finance its expansion plans while strengthening operational capabilities.
"Being listed also provides access to the capital market, giving us greater flexibility to raise funds in the future to support new business opportunities and long-term growth," he told Business Times.
The interior fit-out specialist raised about RM18.1 million from its initial public offering (IPO), with shares priced at 20 sen each.
Proceeds will be channelled towards expanding operations, enhancing digital infrastructure, strengthening working capital and pursuing selective international ventures, particularly in the Philippines and Indonesia.
Of the total, RM6.4 million is earmarked for local expansion, RM3.9 million for working capital and RM3.6 million for listing expenses.
Kan said Adnex will prioritise three key strategies, starting with expanding its footprint in Kuala Lumpur and Selangor, alongside establishing new branch offices in Penang, Johor and Sabah to better serve customers nationwide.
"Second, we plan to enhance operational efficiency by upgrading IT systems, hardware, software, and cloud solutions to support project documentation and a growing workforce.
"We also intend to strengthen its presence in international markets by setting up offices in Manila and Jakarta, recruiting local teams to support business development and project execution," he said.
Kan added that the IPO also opens the door for employees, eligible persons and the Malaysian public to participate in the company's growth through equity ownership.
"Listing on the ACE Market will enhance Adnex's reputation, helping us expand our customer base, strengthen business relationships, and attract and retain talented employees as we continue to grow," he said.
The IPO includes 25 million shares allocated to retail investors, split equally between Bumiputera and non-Bumiputera investors, and 15 million shares reserved for employees and key contributors.
Founded in 2004, Adnex provides end-to-end turnkey interior fit-out solutions across sectors including financial services, manufacturing, food and beverages and pharmaceuticals.
The company recorded RM67.1 million in revenue and a net profit of RM6.48 million in 2025, largely driven by corporate office projects.
With a 2.5 per cent share of Malaysia's RM2 billion interior fit-out industry, Adnex is targeting sustainable growth through strategic expansion and digital upgrades.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: RegSub plan shelved as Bursa governance strengthens: Expert
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Securities Commission's (SC) decision not to proceed with legislation for a Bursa Malaysia regulatory subsidiary (RegSub) reflects growing confidence in the exchange's strengthened internal governance framework, according to an expert.
Economist and adjunct lecturer at Universiti Teknologi Petronas Samirul Ariff Othman said the move suggests functional independence within Bursa may currently be sufficient, although it may not fully replace a separate regulatory structure over the long term.
On March 19, Bursa said the SC had informed it that a dedicated RegSub was not necessary for now, following improvements to its conflict-of-interest safeguards.
Central to these enhancements is the exchange's Regulatory and Conflicts Committee (RACC), which now has a majority of independent external members, including its chairman.
The SC has described the revised structure as "adequate and practical" for Bursa's regulatory responsibilities.
The RegSub proposal, first mooted in 2020, was intended to structurally separate Bursa's regulatory and commercial functions, addressing inherent tensions faced by a self-listed exchange that also acts as market operator and frontline regulator.
It was also aimed at strengthening investor confidence and aligning Malaysia's market governance framework with international best practices.
Samirul, who is alsosenior consultant with Global Asia Consulting, noted that the SC's decision demonstrates that self-regulation can work, but it comes with limits.
"Self-regulation can be effective if internal safeguards are genuinely independent, well-resourced, and backed by strong external oversight.
"But it does not prove that internal arrangements are always equivalent to a fully separate regulatory body," he said.
He added that the SC's move reflects a judgement on Malaysia's current governance improvements, not a rejection of structural separation in principle.
"The conflict RegSub was meant to address has not disappeared. It is now being managed through enhanced governance mechanisms within Bursa," he said.
According to Samirul, international approaches differ.
Singapore has a separate regulatory subsidiary, SGX RegCo, whose board operates independently of SGX's business functions.
Hong Kong, by contrast, relies on dense oversight and conflict-management arrangements for HKEX without a standalone regulatory entity.
"This shows there is no single global template for exchange governance," he noted.
Samirul also said the SC's decision could influence investor perception, though immediate market mechanics are unlikely to change.
Strengthened RACC disclosures signal professionalised conflict management, which may reassure domestic retail investors, he added.
Foreign institutional investors, particularly those focused on governance, may watch more closely.
He said if investors perceive commercial interests influencing enforcement or listing decisions, risk premiums on Malaysian equities could increase, participation may become more selective, and liquidity could move toward markets with stronger governance certainty.
Economically, Samirul said, continuing as both market operator and regulator carries persistent risks.
He noted that profit motives may create pressure to avoid decisions that could reduce trading activity or fee income.
He said perceived unevenness in enforcement could weaken confidence, while questions over regulatory neutrality could affect the broader appeal of Malaysia's capital markets, increase the cost of capital, and shift more oversight responsibilities back to the SC.
Samirul noted that other consequences include reputational vulnerability.
"Bursa is central market infrastructure, and any doubts over its impartiality could affect listings, capital raising, and portfolio allocation.
"Investors might demand higher returns for perceived governance risks, making fundraising more expensive and potentially slowing the flow of savings into productive investments," he said.
Samirul added that the SC's approach favours functional independence over structural separation.
He said the real test is whether Bursa can maintain credible enforcement while operating as a commercial, self-listed exchange.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Oil markets set for extended price pressure: Rystad
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Oil prices are expected to remain elevated for at least six months even if a ceasefire is reached in the US-Iran conflict, as supply disruptions continue to weigh on global energy markets.
Rystad Energy, an independent energy research and business intelligence company based in Norway, said the conflict may conclude within weeks, but its impact on oil supply will linger well beyond any immediate resolution.
The elevated energy prices are putting a strain on the US while Iran faces the risk of losing its control over the country if the war rages on, Rystad chief executive officer and founder Jarand Rystad said.
"Our main view remains that the conflict is most likely to end in the fifth or sixth week since it commenced, therefore in the second week of April. This is also what the energy markets in the world believe, especially with the current energy prices.
"If the ceasefire doesn't happen and the Strait of Hormuz continues to close, we will come into a much more serious energy crisis than we have seen so far," he told Business Times.
The Strait of Hormuz, a critical chokepoint for global oil trade handling roughly a fifth of the world's oil supply, has been partially disrupted by the conflict, triggering supply constraints and pushing prices higher.
Rystad said nearly 15 million barrels per day (bpd) of oil supply has been affected, with several Middle Eastern producers forced to curtail output as storage facilities reach capacity amid restricted exports.
He added that even if a ceasefire is reached, supply recovery will not be immediate, as some oil fields require time to restart operations, potentially keeping up to seven million bpd offline in the near term.
"There will still be too little oil in the market for the next month, regardless of a ceasefire or not. So the oil prices are likely to stay elevated for a while with US$120 being the highest it could reach for now.
"However if the ceasefire does not take place, prices could go much higher," he said.
Rystad also warned that the severity of the disruption has not been fully reflected in global messaging, noting that current supply curtailments are more significant than during the 1973 oil embargo and even the demand shocks seen during the Covid-19 pandemic.
The prolonged supply tightness is expected to keep energy markets volatile in the coming months, adding pressure to inflation and economic growth globally, including in import-dependent economies.
While prices are expected to moderate gradually as supply returns, Rystad said they are unlikely to revert to pre-conflict levels, when the market was in oversupply.
"There was an oversupply in the market prior to the war so we will not reach the previous level. Maybe it will be around US$70 per barrel towards year-end," he added.
DEPLETING STORAGE
Although the war has led to a disruption in oil supply, Rystad said the world was prepared for the shortage thanks to the International Energy Agency (IEA) drive towards energy storage.
IEA requires member countries to maintain emergency oil stockpiles equivalent to at least 90 days of their net oil imports to overcome severe supply disruption.
On top of that, Rystad noted that there was already an oversupply of oil at sea mainly from Russia due to sanctions on its oil shipments by the US.
However, Asian markets are now turning to the readily available Russian oil on sea, reducing the supply amid the ongoing conflict.
"The end-user market has yet to feel the pain from the oil supply disruption because of these storage. But the storage will deplete and we will soon start to feel the impact from the crisis," Rystad said.
BETTER WITHOUT SUBSIDY
Rystad said removing fuel subsidies in Malaysia will develop a "healthy" energy system in the county that allows for better adjustment between supply and demand.
He added that a gradual removal of the subsidy also enables adoption of renewable energy.
"However, for many governments, they don't take that burden to take away something that a population thinks is something they should have a right to have, which in this case is affordable energy.
"But I think at least gradually, the subsidies should be taken away to incentivise a shift towards cleaner and cheaper energy."
Rystad said  the Middle East conflict provides an opportunity for Asian economies to build their energy storage.
It is also crucial for Asian countries to pick up pace on renewable energy adoption.
"From an economic and  energy security perspective, especially amid the Middle East conflict, it will make sense for Asian countries to speed up the pace of renewable development for the next 10 years," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Selangor govt rejects 'no notice' claims in nursery eviction dispute
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Selangor government has refuted claims by a group of nursery operators in Sungai Buloh that there was a lack of communication on their eviction to make way for road upgrading works.
Selangor Infrastructure and Agriculture Committee chairman Datuk Izham Hashim said multiple notices had been issued over the years.
"That is not true. We have proof that notices were issued repeatedly," he said when contacted, responding to claims of insufficient engagement between the state government and the operators.
The operators of 66 nursery plots are seeking an extension to the April 15 eviction deadline, as well as fair compensation and the allocation of a new site, claiming the notice was issued during Ramadan with little prior communication.
In 2022, they were offered relocation to a site in Bukit Changgang, Banting, by the Selangor Agricultural Development Corporation (PKPS), where they drew lots and paid deposits of RM600 per lot, pending the completion of infrastructure.
In 2024, they received a letter asking if they were still interested in relocating to the site, which remained unready. The group reaffirmed their interest.
However, on Feb 19, PKPS withdrew the offer and proposed a one-off ex gratia payment of RM10,000 per lot.
The payment was in line with a decision by the Selangor state executive council on Oct 30, 2025, to provide compensation to all 66 nursery operators as a resolution to the issue and to facilitate the construction of an elevated road.
When asked whether notices were issued following the meeting, Izham replied briefly: "Yes."
He said  notices had been issued several times over the years.
On the proposed relocation site in Bukit Changgang, Izham said efforts had been made to move the operators there, but the area was found to be unsuitable.
"We tried Bukit Changgang, but the area is low-lying and floods frequently, making it unsuitable for nurseries.
"The state government agreed to provide RM10,000 ex gratia to each lot owner. We can no longer delay the vacating of the site as the road upgrading works have already been postponed for quite some time."
On March 14, a portal reported Izham as saying that the state government would not consider any extension to the deadline as road widening projects had been postponed multiple times.
He said the state government was prepared to help the nursery operators, particularly with relocation to new sites, adding that some operators had already moved.
The state government, he said, could help with locating a new site for some operators only and not all of them as a suitable site had yet to be found.
On March 16, Parti Sosialis Malaysia central committee member A. Sivarajan, in a post on X, criticised the 2022 offer to relocate operators to Bukit Changgang, noting that the site was later found to be unsuitable. He urged the state government to halt the eviction until a suitable relocation site could be identified.
In response, Selangor Menteri Besar Datuk Seri Amirudin Shari acknowledged that Bukit Changgang was a low-lying area and prone to flooding.
"The road widening works are important to address traffic congestion. We are offering other alternatives for relocation," he was quoted as saying.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'Choose peace': Pope marks first Easter under cloud of Mideast war
Article:
VATICAN CITY: Pope Leo XIV on Sunday urged "those who have the power to unleash wars" to "choose peace" in his first Easter blessing as pontiff with the Middle East conflict raging.
Christians around the world marked the holiday under the shadow of a war that began with US-Israeli strikes against Iran on Feb 28 and has drawn in the whole region, convulsing the global economy.
"We are growing accustomed to violence, resigning ourselves to it, and becoming indifferent," the pope told a crowd in St Peter's Square.
"Indifferent to the deaths of thousands of people," he added. "Indifferent to the repercussions of hatred and division that conflicts sow."
The leader of the world's 1.4 billion Roman Catholics, who was elected in May 2025, also called for a prayer vigil at the Vatican on April 11.
He paid tribute to his predecessor pope Francis who last appeared in public on Easter Sunday last year – a few hours before his death.
Breaking with a tradition established by his predecessors, he did not specifically directly refer to any country or region caught up in crisis.
Leo has repeatedly called for peace in the Middle East and this week directly urged US President Donald Trump to find an "off-ramp."
On Sunday he spoke of "a world ravaged by wars and marked by a hatred and indifference that make us feel powerless in the face of evil."
- JERUSALEM RESTRICTIONS -
In Jerusalem, there was silence in the alleyways of the Old City, left deserted by Israel's conflict in Gaza and now the Middle East war.
Israeli authorities have severely restricted access to the Church of the Holy Sepulchre, where the faithful commemorate Christ's crucifixion and resurrection, because of security considerations.
On routes approaching the church, police checkpoints screened a small number of worshippers allowed near the site.
All shops in the area were closed, heightening the sense of emptiness.
"It's very hard for all of us because it's our holiday... It's really hard to want to pray but to come here and find nothing. Everything is closed," said Christina Toderas, 44, from Romania.
Otmar Wassermann, 65, had also attempted to enter the Holy Sepulchre but failed.
"I must say I was somewhat frustrated," he told AFP, recalling how the feast is generally celebrated every year.
"The doors are still closed," the Latin Patriarch of Jerusalem, Cardinal Pierbattista Pizzaballa, said in his Easter Vigil homily at the Holy Sepulchre.
Pizzaballa was prevented by Israeli police from holding mass in the church last Sunday, an incident that sparked international indignation.
"The silence is almost absolute, broken perhaps by the distant sound of what war continues to sow in this holy and torn land," he said, according to a text of his sermon issued by his office.
- 'TRUST IN GOD' -
In Lebanon, majority Christian areas in the south of the country are caught in the crossfire between Israel and the Iran-backed Hizbollah movement.
In Debel, close to the Israeli border, inhabitants marked Easter Sunday against the sound of bombardment around their village, now almost totally cut off from the world and dependent on aid deliveries.
"The situation is tragic," town notable Joseph Attieh told AFP by phone.
"People are terrified, and the sound of shelling and gunfire has not stopped for a moment since last night. We haven't been able to sleep.
"We are putting our trust in God," Attieh said, since "this is the only glimmer of hope we will not give up on."
The war has also impacted the lives of Christian minorities in other parts of the Middle East.
In Dubai, masses have been cancelled until further notice as a security precaution. In Damascus, Catholic authorities said Easter celebrations would be restricted to mass following an attack on a Christian town in central Syria.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 600 families tangled up in nurseries' relocation row
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR:  The fate of some 600 families hangs in the balance after 66 nursery  operators in Sungai Buloh  were ordered to vacate land they have occupied since 1999 by April 15.
Operators say the cost of relocating a single nursery lot could reach  RM70,000 — about seven times higher than the RM10,000 ex gratia payment offered by the state government.
They told the
New Straits Times
that relocation expenses included transportation and labour for plants, pots and equipment, land deposits, construction of shade structures, installation of water and electricity supply, as well as the re-establishment of irrigation systems and basic infrastructure.
"All of this can easily cost between RM40,000 and RM70,000 per lot, depending on size. Larger plots could cost even more.
"The RM10,000 ex gratia does not even cover the deposit and basic setup.  And that is before we move a single plant to the new site," said Selangor and Kuala Lumpur Landscaping and Nursery Operators Association president Lee Chee Hoong.
When met in Sungai Buloh, Lee said operators were seeking an extension to the deadline, fair compensation and the allocation of a new site, claiming that the eviction notice was issued during Ramadan with little prior communication.
He said in 2022, operators were offered relocation to a site in Bukit Changgang, Banting, by the Selangor Agricultural Development Corporation (PKPS), where they had drawn lots and paid deposits totalling RM600 per lot, with relocation pending infrastructure.
But in 2024, they received a letter asking if they were still interested in relocating to the site, which was not yet ready. The group reaffirmed their interest.
"After that, there was no communication until Feb 19, when we received a letter cancelling the offer and asking us to collect the RM10,000 ex gratia payment to make way for road widening works," Lee said.
[[nid:1411160]]
On March 16, more than 50 operators were issued notices for unlawful occupation under Section 425 of the National Land Code, giving them 30 days to vacate the land.
Offenders face a fine of up to RM500,000, imprisonment of up to five years, or both, upon conviction.
The nurseries, which occupy a 6km stretch along Jalan Sungai Buloh, began operations in 1999 following an agreement with PKPS to use the land for horticulture.
However, their temporary occupation licences (TOL) were not renewed after 2008 due to the presence of major underground water pipelines.
In 2012, the state government proposed relocating operators to land adjacent to their existing sites, owned by the Malaysian Rubber Board.
Under the plan, endorsed by the then menteri besar, portions of the land would be allocated to nursery operators once development projects commenced, forming a state-level nursery and landscaping hub. It is understood that the plan did not proceed.
In 2019, some operators were asked to relocate to facilitate the construction of the Damansara-Shah Alam Expressway and road-widening works linked to a township development.
According to a letter dated Aug 5, 2020 to Selangor Infrastructure and Agriculture Committee chairman Datuk Izham Hashim, sighted by NST, the relocation was to be carried out in phases: Bukit Cherakah along Persiaran Mokhtar Dahari, Puncak Alam (Phase 1); an adjacent site (Phase 2); HICOM Section 28, Shah Alam (Phase 3); and, a final site designated as a state-level nursery hub (Phase 4).
For Phase 1, six operators were approved for relocation, with their TOL applications endorsed on Nov 5, 2019, but they faced difficulties due to incomplete infrastructure at the site.
In Phase 2, involving eight operators, their relocation  was met with other challenges  due to the lack of tree-felling permits from the Selangor Forestry Department, as well as issues related to land and slope works.
Meanwhile, eight operators at the Sungai Buloh junction were slated for relocation to Hicom Section 28, Shah Alam.
Lee said despite having paid deposits, many operators, including the remaining 43 under Phase 4, have yet to be relocated.
He said over the past years, operators had been cooperative with the state government, including temporarily ceasing operations to make way for development.
He questioned the lack of engagement despite delays in project timelines.
[[nid:1411159]]
"If there has been a delay of 1½ years, why was this not communicated earlier? Why were there no discussions if the project is urgent?
"Even if we move, our children go to school here.
"One lot requires about 10 workers. Sixty lots mean more than 600 families. Where are they supposed to find jobs?"
Lee said the relocation would also affect customers, including those from across the country and overseas.
"We have an established supply chain — wholesale, retail, fertiliser and pot suppliers. Once that is disrupted, it is very difficult to rebuild.
"The economy is not doing well either.
"We are not asking for much, just for the authorities to honour what was promised.
"At the very least, provide a new site," he said adding that they did not mind shifting their nurseries inwards again.
On March 13, the operators, led by Lee, presented a memorandum to Selangor Menteri Besar Datuk Seri Amirudin Shari requesting an alternative site for the relocation.
It was reported that a representative told the group that they might be able to meet Amirudin after Hari Raya Aidilfitri.
The NST has reached out to Amirudin's office for comments.
[[nid:1411158]]
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Level 2 heatwave scorches Kota Setar
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
KUALA LUMPUR: The hot and dry weather continues to batter Kota Setar in Kedah as the district remains under Level 2 heatwave conditions.
According to the Malaysian Meteorological Department (MetMalaysia), Kota Setar was recording daily maximum temperatures ranging from 37°C to 40°C up to 4pm yesterday.
MetMalaysia also reported that 18 areas nationwide, including several in Kedah, were under Level 1 (Alert) with daily highs of 35°C to 37°C.
The affected districts in Kedah are Kuala Muda, Kubang Pasu, Pokok Sena, Pendang, Padang Terap, Sik and Baling.
Similar conditions have been reported in Perlis; North Seberang Prai in Penang; Kuala Kangsar and Hulu Perak in Perak; Jerantut and Maran in Pahang; and Segamat in Johor.
Other areas affected include Kuala Krai, Pasir Mas, Tanah Merah and Machang in Kelantan.
The hot weather is expected to persist for at least three consecutive days. The public can check daily heatwave updates on MetMalaysia's official website.
In Ipoh, the Perak government is monitoring water levels in the state's main dams, which have been declining due to the prolonged heatwave.
State Infrastructure, Energy, Water and Transportation Committee chairman Datuk Seri Mohammad Nizar Jamaluddin told Bernama that the Bukit Merah dam's water level stood at 6.965m, and was now classified as Stage One Critical Irrigation.
The Temenggor dam, he said, had also seen a continuous drop in water levels since early January, with the current level at 239.07m — approaching the dam's safe operational critical level of 238m.
"Overall, dams at critical levels require continuous monitoring, disciplined management of water releases and prudent water usage," said Nizar.
He added that in general, the state's water supply remained stable and under control.
"All water treatment plants are operating efficiently at the required production levels, except for those facing raw water shortages at the intake points."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Oil jumps above US$111 as Middle East war rattles markets
Article:
TOKYO: Oil prices opened higher on Monday after the Easter holiday weekend as the US-Israeli war with Iran continues to disrupt global oil supply.
Brent crude futures rose US$2.4, or 2.2 per cent, to US$111.43 a barrel by 2215 GMT. US West Texas Intermediate crude futures gained US$3, or 2.7 per cent, to trade at US$114.57 per barrel.
US President Donald Trump on Sunday ratcheted up pressure on Iran, threatening in an expletive-laden Easter Sunday social media post to target Iran's power plants and bridges on Tuesday if the strategic Strait of Hormuz is not reopened.--REUTERS
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Cheri, Rambai help Perhilitan relocate wild bull elephant
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
TWO decoy elephants, Cheri and Rambai, were deployed during an operation in Felda Aring 10 here to lure a male elephant into relocation.
The wild bull, nicknamed "Man 10", was captured by the Wildlife and National Parks Department (Perhilitan) on Wednesday, bringing relief to about 500 residents in nearby villages.
Resident Khaidir Ibrahim, 39, said human-elephant conflict in the area had persisted for nearly two years, leaving the community in constant fear.
He said the animals frequently strayed into the settlement and destroyed oil palm saplings and ornamental plants in residents' gardens.
"There have been sightings for almost two years.
"But in the past three months, the elephants became more active near our homes.
"Alhamdullilah, Perhilitan has captured two wild bulls roaming Felda Aring 10 and 11.
"While this has eased our concerns, we know other wild elephants still roam the plantations," he said yesterday.
Villager Ainina Afiqah Mohamad Rotik, 29, said the elephants not only damaged banana trees and landscaping, but also posed a growing risk to safety.
She said residents avoided going out at night due to this.
"Previously, an elephant damaged a garage and entered house compounds. It was frightening as it used paths right next to our homes.
"We are relieved and grateful that this elephant has been captured and relocated.
"We thank Perhilitan for acting swiftly to ensure the safety of workers and residents," Ainina added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Iran warns Trump 'whole region going to burn'
Article:
TEHERAN: Iran's powerful parliament speaker warned US President Donald Trump on Sunday that his "reckless moves" would mean "our whole region is going to burn."
"Your reckless moves are dragging the United States into a living hell for every single family, and our whole region is going to burn because you insist on following Netanyahu's commands," Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf wrote in an X post in English, referring to Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu.
Ghalibaf added that the "only real solution is respecting the rights of the Iranian people and ending this dangerous game."--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: PNB Merdeka launches inaugural Jelajah Warisan KL
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
KUALA LUMPUR: Permodalan Nasional Bhd's (PNB) subsidiary, PNB Merdeka Ventures Sdn Bhd, has launched the first Jelajah Warisan KL at Stadium Merdeka.
The citywide series is aimed at promoting public engagement with Kuala Lumpur's heritage.
The initiative, part of the broader Warisan Kuala Lumpur (Warisan KL) agenda, involves government agencies, government-linked companies, government-linked investment companies, heritage partners and local communities.
It seeks to highlight the historical and cultural significance of key areas while aligning with Madani Economy principles such as inclusivity and shared prosperity.
Menara Merdeka 118 is the first stop in the series, with site walkthroughs showcasing the precinct's conservation efforts, cultural milestones and ongoing transformation, as well as its role in community life and tourism.
PNB Merdeka chief executive officer Datuk Izwan Ibrahim said Jelajah Warisan KL is more than just a guided walk.
It also serves as an invitation for partners and communities to rediscover, reconnect and embrace the spaces that define the capital's story.
"By experiencing these heritage sites together, we cultivate a shared sense of pride and responsibility. This collective ownership is vital to sustaining the legacy of the Merdeka 118 precinct and ensuring its relevance for generations to come," he said in a statement.
The initiative also highlights how Kuala Lumpur's historical landmarks, such as Stadium Merdeka, can coexist harmoniously with modern developments like Merdeka 118.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trump warns Iran: 'Open the Hormuz Strait or face hell' after airman rescue
Article:
WASHINGTON: US President Donald Trump made an expletive-filled threat Sunday to destroy Iran's vital civilian infrastructure, demanding Teheran bow to his demands for a deal to reopen the Gulf to shipping and end the Middle East war.
As Christians marked Easter, the US leader revived warnings of air strikes on Iranian power plants and bridges, after celebrating the rescue of a wounded airman whose F-15 fighter jet went down inside Iran.
Iranian images showed wreckage of several aircraft, but Teheran did not deny US forces rescued the officer, who sheltered in a mountainous area while American special forces and Iranian troops raced to find him.
The war, which erupted on February 28 with deadly US-Israeli strikes on Teheran, has engulfed the Middle East and convulsed the global economy.
Iranian missiles have hit Israeli cities and economic infrastructure in the Gulf, sending world energy prices soaring.
Iran has also effectively blocked the Strait of Hormuz, a vital shipping lane for oil and gas, provoking Trump to demand Sunday: "Open the F*****' Strait, you crazy bastards, or you'll be living in hell."
Later, in a terse follow-up post, the president apparently set a new deadline for Iran to comply: "Tuesday, 8:00 PM" (midnight GMT).
- 'NOTHING LIKE IT!!!' -
"Tuesday will be Power Plant Day, and Bridge Day, all wrapped up in one, in Iran. There will be nothing like it!!!" he declared, drawing a rebuke from Teheran, which accused Trump of taking orders from Israel's Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu.
"Your reckless moves are dragging the United States into a living he;; for every single family, and our whole region is going to burn because you insist on following Netanyahu's commands," Iran's parliament speaker, Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf, posted on social media.
Iran's ally Russia also condemned Trump's threat.
Moscow said Washington should abandon "the language of ultimatums" and return to negotiations, according to a Russian readout of Foreign Minister Sergei Lavrov's latest call with his Iranian counterpart.
Many residents of Teheran seemed indifferent to Trump's invective, with young Iranians exercising, flying kites and holding picnics in a large park in the city's west Sunday.
- US RESCUE MISSION -
US media reported on details of the rescue of the US airman, a weapons systems officer. The New York Times said he was equipped with a pistol, a beacon and a secure communications device to coordinate with rescuers.
Two of the aircraft meant to transport him and his rescuers to safety were stuck in a remote base in Iran and had to be destroyed to prevent them from falling into Iranian hands, The Times and CBS reported.
US forces then used three other transport planes to carry the airman and his rescuers out of Iran, the reports said.
Iran's military said it had destroyed four US aircraft involved in the operation, which it said had made use of an abandoned airport in southern Isfahan province.
Iranian media reported five people were killed in strikes during the operation.
Footage released by Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps was presented as showing charred, smoking wreckage of an American aircraft scattered across a desert area.
Iran has said its forces downed the fighter jet and the crew ejected, while US media reported only that the plane had been shot down. Washington has not confirmed what caused the plane to come down.
- 'DEATHS OF THOUSANDS' -
Critical infrastructure across the Gulf came under attack from Iran again on Sunday, with damage reported at civilian facilities in the United Arab Emirates, Bahrain and Kuwait.
UAE authorities in Sharjah said they were dealing with an "incident" in the key port of Khor Fakkan following an Iranian strike.
UAE presidential adviser Anwar Gargash warned Iran that targeting its Arab neighbours "will actually concretise the American role... It will not reduce it.
"We will also see Israeli influence become more prominent in the Gulf, not less," he added, confirming the UAE was ready to "join any American-led effort, international effort to secure navigation in the Strait of Hormuz."
On another front, Lebanon has increasingly been drawn into the conflict since the Iran-backed Hizbollah group began targeting Israel.
Israel has struck back and pushed its ground forces into southern Lebanon, with the army chief Lieutenant General Eyal Zamir visiting troops there Sunday and pledging to intensify strikes against Hizbollah.
UN peacekeepers in Lebanon have warned that attacks by Israel and Hizbollah near its positions "could potentially draw return fire."
The Israeli military and medics said a missile fired from Iran hit a residential building in the northern city of Haifa, injuring four people.
The war has cast a shadow over Easter Sunday celebrations for Christians in Lebanon and across the region.
The usually lively alleyways of Jerusalem's Old City were unusually silent Sunday.
Israeli authorities restricted access to the Holy Sepulchre, where worshippers commemorate Christ's crucifixion and resurrection.
"It's very hard for all of us because it's our holiday... It's really hard to want to pray but to come here and find nothing. Everything is closed," said Christina Toderas, 44, from Romania.
In Beirut, hymn singers at a church in the northern suburb of Jdeideh struggled to be heard above the roar of Israeli fighter jets flying low over the city and bombing its southern suburbs.
In his Easter blessing at the Vatican, Pope Leo XIV urged "those who have the power to unleash wars" to "choose peace" instead and criticised global indifference to "the deaths of thousands."--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Taiwan opposition leader to make 'peace' visit to China
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
TAIPEI: Taiwan's main opposition leader will make a rare trip to China tomorrow, weeks before US President Donald Trump — with Beijing expected to use the visit to increase its influence over the democratic island.
Cheng Li-wun, who will become the first sitting chairperson of the Kuomintang (KMT) to travel to China in a decade, said she wanted to meet Chinese President Xi Jinping to build cross-strait "peace".
Taiwanese officials and experts, however, say Xi sees an opportunity to reinforce Cheng's standing in the KMT and stymie further US weapons sales to Taiwan.
The KMT has long advocated for closer relations with China, which claims Taiwan is part of its territory and has threatened to use force to seize it.
But Cheng, whose unexpected rise to the top of the KMT drew congratulations from Xi last October, has been accused by critics, including inside the party, of being too pro-China.
Cheng's visit comes as the United States — Taiwan's most important security backer — intensifies pressure on Taiwanese opposition lawmakers to approve a proposal for defence purchases, including billions of dollars worth of US weapons, to deter a potential Chinese attack.
The KMT leader, who has railed against the government's T$1.25 trillion plan, faces deepening divisions inside her party over how to counter China's military threats.
Cheng backed a KMT proposal to allocate T$380 billion for US weapons with the option for more acquisitions, but more moderate senior figures in the party are pushing for a much higher budget.
Beijing sees "a need to rescue Cheng Li-wun" from a "power crisis", said Albert Tzeng, a former KMT adviser.
Xi's endorsement of Cheng will make her critics wary of attacking her, Tzeng said.
And Xi, who has tied taking Taiwan to his vision of the "great rejuvenation of the Chinese nation", can show Beijing is "not losing Taiwan altogether to the US", he added.
Taiwan's top China policy body warned that Beijing had "summoned" Cheng for the purpose of cutting off "Taiwan's military purchases from the US and cooperation with other countries". — AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Iran internet blackout is longest shutdown on record - NetBlocks
Article:
PARIS: Iran's internet blackout, which was imposed soon after the US and Israel attacked the country on Feb 28, is now the longest nationwide shutdown on record, according to the monitor NetBlocks.
"Iran's internet blackout is now the longest nation-scale internet shutdown on record in any country, exceeding all other comparable incidents in severity having entered its 37th consecutive day after 864 hours," NetBlocks said in a tweet.
In another tweet, the monitor noted some countries had experienced intermittent or regional-level shutdowns over longer periods, while North Korea had never been connected to the global internet at all.
"We constantly find ourselves searching for ways to reconnect, just to be able to hear reliable news," a 47-year-old woman in the central city of Isfahan told AFP Saturday.
"Being without internet feels like being without oxygen to me. I feel trapped and suffocated," a 53-year-old man in Teheran said.
Iran's extremely restricted local internet has been working throughout the war, enabling people to connect to domestic websites.
To connect to the international internet for news or banned social media networks such as Instagram, some are managing to use virtual private networks (VPNs) to disguise their origins in rare moments when network is available.
Iranians suspected of using VPNs since the war began have received text messages warning them of arrest or imprisonment.
Far more limited numbers of people have access to Starlink or other satellite-based internet providers, which are also banned.
Iranians were previously placed under an 18-day internet blackout in January amid anti-government protests during which thousands were killed. - AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Four Daim's children to give statements at MACC today
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Four children of former finance minister the late Tun Daim Zainuddin are scheduled to give statements at the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) headquarters later today.
It is learnt that Asnida, Wira Dani, Amir Zainuddin, and Amin Zainuddin are scheduled to provide statements to MACC officers about 9.30am.
A senior MACC officer, who confirmed the matter, said the process of recording their statements will begin today.
However, he declined to comment further.
It was previously reported that the MACC had issued a notice to trace Daim's four children following their failure to respond and non-compliance with two official notices served by the commission regarding asset declarations.
According to the MACC, the notices were issued after no response was received and attempts to contact all four had failed as of Feb 26.
The MACC said the children's lawyers had previously informed them that their clients were abroad and had also faced difficulties in making contact.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Police hunt group of masked men over Cheras entertainment centre riot [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Police are hunting a group of men suspected of causing a commotion at an entertainment centre in Cheras here yesterday.
Kuala Lumpur police chief Datuk Fadil Marsus said a report was lodged around 7.30pm on the same day the incident occurred, believed to have happened at about 1.30pm.
"The suspects reportedly rammed the premises' gate with a lorry, forced their way inside, and vandalised property," he said in a statement.
The case is being investigated under Sections 148 and 427 of the Penal Code for rioting with armed weapons and mischief, respectively.
A 60-second clip circulating online showed a lorry reversing and crashing into the gate of the premises, before a group of men, each covering their faces with skull caps and armed with unidentified objects, broke into the premises.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Man arrested for attacking motorcyclist in road rage incident in Kulai
Article:
KULAI: A man has been arrested for allegedly assaulting a motorcyclist in a road rage incident at Bulatan Lebuh Putra Utama, Bandar Putra, here on April 3.
Kulai district police chief, Assistant Commissioner Tan Seng Lee, said the incident occurred around 5.45pm when the 37-year-old victim was on his way to work in Singapore.
He said that the Kulai district police headquarters had received a report on the case, after a video showing a man being struck by another individual circulated on social media.
"Preliminary investigations indicate that the suspect was driving recklessly and abruptly changed lanes, nearly colliding with the victim's motorcycle. The victim then tapped on the suspect's car window, which appeared to anger the driver.
"The suspect suddenly stopped his vehicle in front of the victim's motorcycle in the middle of the road, forcing the motorcycle to brake abruptly. He then allegedly kicked the motorcycle until it toppled over and struck the victim on the head and body with his hands, before other road users intervened to break up the fight," he said in a statement today.
Tan said the motorcyclist sustained minor injuries from the incident.
Acting on the information, a team of officers from the Kulai district police headquarters arrested the suspect at his residence in Senai yesterday at 11.30pm.
"The suspect has been remanded for four days from April 5 to 8 to assist in investigations. Members of the public with information on this incident are urged to contact the police hotline at 07-6632222 or reach out directly to the investigating officer, Inspector Teo Feng Shun, at 017-7759106," he added.
The case is being investigated under Section 279 of the Penal Code for reckless driving, which carries a jail term of up to six months, a maximum fine of RM2,000, or both.
Investigations are also being conducted under Section 323 for causing hurt intentionally, which carries a jail term of up to one year, a maximum fine of RM2,000, or both; Section 506 for criminal intimidation, carrying a jail term of up to two years, a fine, or both; and Section 427 for mischief causing damage to a motorcycle, which carries a jail term of between one and five years, a fine, or both.
"The public is advised not to speculate on the case in ways that could jeopardise the police investigation," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'Hallucination' flaw in AI tools may be unfixable
Article:
HUNDREDS of billions of dollars are riding on the assumption that artificial intelligence will be reliable enough for high-stakes work. New research suggests it may never be.
The artificial intelligence tools that power ChatGPT and its rivals — known as large language models, or LLMs — are a genuine productivity-enhancing innovation.
But they have serious shortcomings, most notably, their tendency to hallucinate, or make things up.
New research on this widely studied phenomenon indicates that hallucinations occur far more often than most people realise, and the issue gets worse the more information you give the AI model to work with.
A new experiment shows just how serious the problem may be.
JV Roig, an expert in agentic AI at the start-up Kamiwaza AI, tested different LLMs in a controlled environment.
He gave each model input text of 32,000, 128,000 or — when possible — 200,000 words.
Then he asked the models to answer questions about these texts. Because the training material was known in advance, he could measure exactly how often the models hallucinated.
At 32,000 words, the best-performing model — China's Zhipu AI's GLM 4.5 — hallucinated just 1.2 per cent of the time. The average was 6.8 per cent.
When the input was pushed to 128,000 words, GLM 4.5's error rate rose to 3.2 per cent, while the average jumped to 10 per cent.
That is a meaningful error rate given the relatively limited input. For those models that allowed training texts of 200,000 words, hallucination rates skyrocketed.
Some broke down entirely, hallucinating answers in the majority of cases.
Of course, in the real world, LLMs aren't trained on a relatively limited set of words but effectively on the whole Internet, which means hallucinations are likely an even bigger problem in reality than in this experiment.
But won't this issue simply be fixed as the technology improves? Current evidence suggests that might not be the case.
In December, a team from Tsinghua University in Beijing published a paper tracing hallucinations to a tiny fraction —  typically less than 0.1 per cent  of an LLM's neurons, the basic decision-making units that process information and pass signals forward, much like switches in an electrical circuit.
That's good news, right?
In an ideal world, one would simply identify the faulty neurons, remove them and fix the problem. Alas, the study indicates this is impossible.
The problematic neurons are formed during the model's initial training, not at a later stage during fine-tuning.
The authors of the study argue that these hallucinating neurons "originate from the inherent characteristics of the next-token prediction objective. This training paradigm does not distinguish between factually correct and incorrect continuations —  it merely rewards fluent text generation".
What all that jargon means is that LLMs are inherently probabilistic, not deterministic. They try to guess the answer that sounds best, not the objectively correct one.
And that's not a problem to be fixed. It's fundamental to how these models work.
These findings should give investors and executives pause —  especially when it comes to throwing lots of money at LLMs and generative AI.
These models are offering "about right" results. In many everyday tasks, like designing a marketing campaign or drafting internal reports, that may be fine.
But in many of the most sophisticated AI software applications, like accounting and law, "about right" is the same as wrong —  and could potentially expose the user to significant legal and financial risk.
Indeed, a March investigation by the
New York Times
found that LLMs asked to fill out US tax forms produced errors serious enough to constitute tax evasion, if not tax fraud, in an Internal Revenue Service  audit.
And these were relatively simple personal tax returns. Business filings are far more complex
Any company using LLMs to fully replace professional accounting software could thus be taking on serious risk.
The same is likely true for other mission-critical functions like risk management.
One could argue that these findings simply show that AI models will always require human oversight, which is something AI firms would probably agree with.
But if too much checking and fixing is required, investors may need to rethink all those projections for massive efficiency gains.
Technology is not standing still, of course. The industry is seeking workarounds to the hallucination problem.
Writing for Reuters, the writer is an investment strategist for Panmure Liberum
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: GOF detains 22 undocumented migrants in Tumpat and Pasir Mas
Article:
TUMPAT: The General Operations Force (GOF) has detained 22 undocumented migrants in two separate operations in Tumpat and Pasir Mas on Friday and Saturday.
GOF Southeast Brigade commander Senior Assistant Ahmad Radzi Hussain said the group comprised 20 nationals from Myanmar and two from Thailand. The Myanmar nationals were arrested on April 4, while the Thai nationals were detained on April 3.
"Acting on intelligence, the GOF team from the Ninth Battalion raided a house in Chabang Empat, Tumpat, at 1.15pm on Saturday. During the raid, they detained 20 Myanmar nationals — 14 men and six women — in the living room of the premises," he said.
Radzi added that all suspects failed to produce valid travel documents and were believed to have entered Malaysia through illegal routes from Thailand.
"Initial investigations indicate that the group had used a human trafficking syndicate, travelling from Myanmar to Thailand before being smuggled into Malaysia via riverine routes. They are believed to have paid around RM8,000 each to agents to work as construction labourers in Malaysia," he said.
All 20 detainees were handed over to the Tumpat district police station for further investigation under Section 6(1)(c) of the Immigration Act 1959/1963.
In a separate case on April 3, Radzi said two Thai teenagers were detained at Kampung Kedap, Rantau Panjang, Pasir Mas, at 6.15pm.
"The team spotted a man and a woman behaving suspiciously while walking from an illegal jetty area in Kampung Kedap. Upon inspection, both failed to produce valid travel documents to enter Malaysia," he said.
Radzi added that the suspects, aged 17 and 16, admitted to working in Malaysia since early 2024 as cooks at a food premises in Pasir Gudang, Johor. They were later taken to the GOF Tactical Command Post in Taman Bakti before being handed over to the Pasir Mas district police station for further action.
The case is being investigated under Sections 5(2) and 6(1)(c) of the Immigration Act 1959/1963.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Court session tomorrow crucial for Indira Gandhi to reunite with daughter
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: M. Indira Gandhi and her lawyers will return to the Ipoh High Court in Perak tomorrow in her 17-year legal battle to reunite with her daughter.
Tomorrow has been fixed for a judicial monitoring session, which was initiated in 2020 to compel the police to appear in court and provide an update every three months on their progress in locating her daughter, Prasana Diksa, and arresting her ex-husband, Muhammad Riduan Abdullah.
It is also an important day for Indira as her daughter will officially turn 1
8 two days later on April 8
, making her an adult in the eyes of the law.
The 51-year-old tuition teacher will then lose all legal avenues under the Child Act 2001 to compel the authorities to reunite her with her daughter, who was abducted by her ex-husband when the girl was only 11 months old.
"As a mother, I am hoping for a miracle," Indira had previously told the New Straits Times as she pinned her hopes on her daughter coming out to look for her on her own.
Inspector-General of Police Datuk Seri
Mohd Khalid Ismail
had previously pledged his commitment to assist in resolving the case.
"We will do our best within our responsibility."
Meanwhile, Indira Gandhi Action Team chairman Arun Dorasamy described tomorrow's judicial monitoring proceedings as significant in Indira's bid to reunite with Prasana.
"Prasana will turn 18 — the age of majority under the law. As such, committal proceedings to enforce the child's return can no longer continue in the same manner.
"The right to determine her future will rest entirely in her own hands," he said in a statement today.
"We will continue to hold on to one hope. That Prasana will be reunited with her mother. That justice, though delayed, can still be upheld.
"The rule of law cannot exist merely on paper. It must be enforced. Justice delayed too long is justice denied," he added.
Indira's ordeal began in 2009, when she filed a legal challenge against the unilateral conversion of her three children to Islam by her ex-husband.
She was also granted custody of all her children in 2010, which her ex-husband failed to comply and absconded with Prasana.
The Ipoh High Court quashed the children's conversion in 2013, but the Court of Appeal reversed the decision in 2015.
Finally, in January 2018, the Federal Court nullified the unilateral conversion of the three children, including Prasana's, ruling that the word "parent" in Article 12(4) of the Federal Constitution must be interpreted in the plural sense to mean both parents, if both were alive.
But Indira never got the chance to see her daughter ever since she was taken in 2010.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trump's anger over Iran war thrusts Nato into fresh crisis
Article:
THE Nato alliance has in recent years survived existential challenges —  ranging from the war in Ukraine to multiple bouts of pressure and insults from US President Donald Trump, who has questioned its core mission and threatened to seize Greenland.
But it is the US-Israeli war with Iran that has nearly broken the 76-year-old bloc and threatens to leave it in its weakest state since its creation, analysts and diplomats say.
Trump — enraged that European countries have declined to send their navies to open up the Strait of Hormuz to global shipping following the start of the air war on Feb 28 — has declared he is considering withdrawing from the alliance.
In a speech  last week, Trump criticised US allies but stopped short of condemning Nato.
Combined with other barbs aimed at Europeans in recent weeks, Trump's comments have provoked unprecedented concern that the United States will not come to the aid of European allies should they be attacked, whether or not Washington formally walks away.
"This is the worst place (Nato) has been since it was founded," said Max Bergmann, a former State Department official who now leads the Europe, Russia, and Eurasia Programme at the Centre for Strategic and International Studies in Washington.
"It's really hard to think of anything that even comes close."
That reality is sinking in for Europeans, who have counted on Nato as a bulwark against an increasingly assertive Russia.
As recently as February, Nato Secretary-General Mark Rutte had dismissed the idea of Europe defending itself without the US as a "silly thought". Now, many officials and diplomats consider it the default expectation.
"Nato remains necessary, but we must be capable of thinking of Nato without the Americans," General Francois Lecointre, who served as France's armed forces chief from 2017 to 2021, said.
Nato has been challenged before, not least during Trump's first term from 2017 to 2021, when he also considered withdrawing from the alliance.
US officials have declared Nato cannot be a "one-way street".
European officials counter that they have not received US requests for specific assets for a mission to open the strait and complain that Washington has been inconsistent about whether such a mission would operate during or after the war.
Trump's latest comments follow other signs of an increasingly unsteady alliance.
Those include his stepped-up threats in January to wrest Greenland away from Denmark and recent moves by the US that Europeans see as particularly accommodating toward Russia, which Nato defines as its principal security threat.
At a meeting of G7 foreign ministers near Paris  in late March, US Secretary of State Marco Rubio and Kaja Kallas, the foreign policy chief of the European Union, had a tense exchange, according to five people familiar with the matter, underlining the increasingly fraught transatlantic relationship.
Legally, Trump may lack the authority to withdraw from Nato.
Under a law passed in 2023, a US president cannot exit the alliance without the consent of two-thirds of the US Senate, a nearly impossible threshold.
But analysts say that as commander-in-chief, Trump can decide whether the US military will defend Nato members.
Declining to do so could imperil the alliance without a formal withdrawal.
To be sure, not everyone sees the current crisis as existential. One French diplomat described the president's rhetoric as a passing temper tantrum.
This week, Nato chief Rutte, who has a strong relationship with Trump, is set to visit Washington in an effort to change Trump's view once again.
European nations have good reason to keep the US engaged in Nato despite doubts over whether Trump would come to their defence.
Among other reasons, the US military provides a range of capabilities that Nato can't easily replace, such as satellite intelligence.
Even if Trump and the Europeans find a way to stay together in Nato, the transatlantic alliance that has been central to the global order since World War 2 may never be the same.
"I do think we're turning the page of 80 years of working together," said Julianne Smith, the US ambassador to Nato under Democratic President Joe Biden.
"I don't think it means the end of the transatlantic relationship, but we're on the cusp of something that's going to have a different look and feel to it."
The writers are from Reuters
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: NST Leader: Of healthcare and public-private sector integration
Article:
COME July, Malaysia's healthcare will pioneer a "whole-of-nation" approach, with private hospitals nationwide being invited to be "Tier 1" providers under the Malaysia Health Insurance Transformation (MHIT) base plan.
"Pioneer" is a word used advisedly. Historically, these two sectors have operated largely in silos, with the public sector bearing the burden of universal coverage and the private sector catering to those who can afford it.
MHIT is making a shift in the relationship between the public and private sectors. The Health Ministry's invitation of private hospitals as Tier 1 providers signals a vital step towards a whole-of-nation approach.
The public-private healthcare sector integration has several upsides.
Firstly, it will help ease, if not solve, overcrowding in public hospitals. If media reports are right, overcrowding is still an issue at certain public hospitals across Malaysia, not just in densely populated Klang Valley.
In Sabah, wait times are said to be two days or beyond. Sarawak is said to face persistent manpower shortage leading to patients being put on wait list at the emergency departments.
Cities like Kuala Lumpur, Penang and Johor Baru report packed emergency departments with patients waiting at least a day before being admitted.
One reason is the doctor-to-patient ratio. Secondly, the integration helps maintain the public healthcare system as the "bedrock" so it is not weakened by privatisation. For some peculiar reasons, governments tend to privatise public services because of shortage of funds.
Privatisation isn't always the answer as many countries have discovered. Some are making them "public" again after seeing many such services fail miserably.
Even free-marketers say public healthcare functions poorly in the free market. In the case of Malaysia, MHIT may make privatisation unnecessary, though a deeper dive may disclose other avenues.
Taking public services like healthcare private is signalling to the people that the service is only meant to serve the wealthy.
Finally, the collaboration between the public and private sectors has the potential to lead to a more equitable distribution of medical expertise across the country.
Potential, yes, but whether it will truly lead to a more equitable distribution of medical expertise remains to be seen.
One of the silent crises in Malaysian healthcare is the migration of experienced specialists from public hospitals to private ones, drawn by better pay and lighter workloads.
By designating private hospitals as Tier 1 providers under a national insurance plan, the government creates a mechanism where private infrastructure can be used to treat "public-interest" cases.
So, instead of a specialist leaving a public hospital to serve only the wealthy, they stay within the system where the MHIT base plan allows a broader demographic to access their services.
However, the success of this policy hinges on the government's ability to  use the private sector to augment public capacity without replacing the public sector's responsibility to provide universal, free-at-point-of-service care for the B40.
The message to the private sector appears to be that "you are no longer a business; you are a critical component of national security and social stability".
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Over 70 feared missing after migrant boat capsizes in Mediterranean
Article:
ROME: Dozens of people are missing after a migrant boat capsized in the central Mediterranean, the NGOs Mediterranea Saving Humans and Sea-Watch said Sunday on social media.
Two people died and 32 were rescued from the boat, which had left Libya on Saturday afternoon with around 105 people on board, according to Mediterranea Saving Humans.
"Tragic Easter shipwreck. 32 survivors, two bodies recovered and more than 70 people missing," the NGO wrote on X, adding that the boat capsized in a search-and-rescue zone handled by Libyan authorities.
Sea-Watch said two commercial ships saved the survivors and took them to the Italian island of Lampedusa.
An aerial video it posted showed two men clinging to the hull of the capsized vessel, and the approach of one of the commercial ships.
Mediterranea Saving Humans said the accident was "the consequence of policies by European governments that refuse to open safe and legal pathways" for migrants.
Lampedusa is a key entry point for migrants crossing the Mediterranean from North Africa to Europe.
Since the start of 2026, at least 683 migrants have lost their lives or gone missing on attempts to cross the sea, according to the UN's migration agency IOM.
According to the Italian government, 6,175 migrants arrived on Italian territory over the same period. - AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Sibu police seize drugs worth over RM164k, arrest Indonesian man
Article:
SIBU: Police have arrested an Indonesian man and seized drugs worth RM164,695 in an integrated operation across the town over the weekend.
Sibu police chief, Assistant Commissioner of Police Azari Miskon, said the 25-year-old suspect was detained at 11.30pm yesterday along Jalan Padawan, where authorities recovered 272.02g of powder suspected to be ecstasy and 2.78g of ketamine.
